title: Box breathing to calm stress
content: Box breathing is a simple technique that can calm the body’s stress response. Inhale for 4 counts, hold for 4, exhale for 4, hold for 4, and repeat for a few rounds. If you feel light‑headed, return to normal breathing and try again more gently.

title: 5-4-3-2-1 grounding for anxious moments
content: Grounding brings attention back to the present when worries spiral. Name 5 things you can see, 4 you can feel, 3 you can hear, 2 you can smell, and 1 you can taste. This can reduce panic sensations and help you feel more in control.

title: Journaling to process feelings
content: Journaling can help you notice patterns in thoughts and emotions without judging yourself. Try writing: “What am I feeling right now?”, “What do I need?”, and “What’s one small step that could help today?”. Short, consistent entries are often more helpful than long ones.

title: When you need urgent support
content: If you feel unsafe, are thinking about harming yourself, or can’t cope right now, seek urgent help. In Canada, you can call or text 9-8-8 for immediate support. If there is immediate danger, call emergency services.

title: How to bring up mental health with a provider
content: It can help to be direct and specific: describe what you’re feeling, how long it’s been happening, and how it affects sleep, appetite, or daily functioning. You can also ask about therapy, community resources, and safety planning. You deserve to be taken seriously.

title: Anxiety during pregnancy is common
content: Many people experience increased worry during pregnancy—about the baby’s health, medical appointments, or big life changes. Signs can include racing thoughts, tension, irritability, or trouble sleeping. Gentle coping skills and support from trusted people or professionals can make a big difference.

title: Depression during pregnancy
content: Depression can show up as persistent sadness, loss of interest, hopelessness, low energy, or changes in sleep and appetite. It is not a personal failure, and support is available. If symptoms last more than two weeks or feel overwhelming, talking to a healthcare professional is recommended.

title: Panic-like symptoms and what can help
content: Panic symptoms can feel intense—rapid heartbeat, shortness of breath, trembling, or fear that something is terribly wrong. While the sensations are scary, they often peak and pass. Slow breathing, grounding, and reaching out to a trusted person can help you ride the wave.

title: Intrusive thoughts in the perinatal period
content: Intrusive thoughts are unwanted thoughts or images that pop into your mind and cause distress. Having a thought is not the same as wanting to act on it. If intrusive thoughts feel frequent, upsetting, or are paired with urges to act, seek professional support promptly.

title: Sleep changes and emotional health
content: Sleep disruption is common in pregnancy and can amplify anxiety and low mood. Helpful basics include consistent sleep/wake times, a wind-down routine, limiting late caffeine, and reducing screens before bed. If insomnia is severe or persistent, ask a provider about safe options.

title: What stress is and why it feels physical
content: Stress is a natural response to challenges. Your body may react with muscle tension, faster breathing, stomach discomfort, or restlessness. Noticing early signs lets you use coping skills sooner—like breathing, movement, hydration, and talking with support.

title: A simple mindfulness reset
content: Mindfulness doesn’t require “emptying your mind.” Try this: notice your breath for 3 slow cycles, then name one thing you see, one thing you feel, and one thing you hear. The goal is to gently return attention to the present—not to force anxiety away.

title: Self-compassion when you feel you’re ‘not doing enough’
content: Pregnancy can bring pressure and self-criticism. A self-compassion pause can help: acknowledge the difficulty, remind yourself you’re not alone, and speak to yourself as you would to a friend. Small kindnesses—rest, food, hydration—are valid care.

title: Building a support network
content: Support can include a partner, friend, family member, doula, midwife, doctor, counselor, or peer group. Be specific about what helps: a check-in text, a walk together, help with chores, or coming to an appointment. People often want to help but need clear requests.

title: Peer support groups
content: Peer support can reduce isolation by connecting you with others who understand pregnancy and postpartum mental health challenges. Groups are not a replacement for medical care, but they can provide validation, coping ideas, and a sense of community.

title: Feeling nervous before an ultrasound
content: It’s common to feel anxious before scans or test results. Consider writing down questions ahead of time, bringing a support person if possible, and using breathing exercises in the waiting room. If anxiety feels intense, tell your provider—you’re allowed to ask for reassurance and clarity.

title: Worry about glucose screening
content: Screening tests can feel stressful because they come with uncertainty. It may help to focus on what you can control: hydration, a plan for the day, and asking how results will be shared. Remember that screening is designed to catch concerns early so you can get support if needed.

title: Making prenatal visits less stressful
content: Before appointments, note symptoms, questions, and any mood changes you want to mention. During the visit, ask what is normal, what is not, and when to follow up. Afterward, a short debrief with someone you trust can help you feel grounded.

title: Morning sickness and nausea
content: Nausea is common in early pregnancy. Some people find small frequent meals, bland foods, ginger, and staying hydrated helpful. If you can’t keep fluids down, feel dizzy, or symptoms are severe, contact a healthcare professional for guidance.

title: Fatigue during pregnancy
content: Fatigue is common due to hormonal changes, sleep disruption, and physical demands. Gentle movement, hydration, iron-rich foods (as advised), and rest breaks can help. If fatigue is extreme or paired with shortness of breath, fainting, or chest pain, seek medical advice.

title: Back and pelvic discomfort
content: Back discomfort can increase as posture and ligaments change. Helpful options include gentle stretching, supportive footwear, heat packs, and prenatal-friendly strengthening (if approved by your provider). Seek medical guidance if pain is severe, sudden, or limits mobility.

title: Heartburn and digestion changes
content: Heartburn can worsen as pregnancy progresses. Some people benefit from smaller meals, avoiding lying down right after eating, and elevating the head of the bed. Ask your provider which medications are safe if lifestyle steps aren’t enough.

title: Headaches and stress
content: Headaches can be triggered by stress, dehydration, low sleep, or changes in routine. Try water, a snack, rest in a dim room, and gentle breathing. If headaches are severe, persistent, or accompanied by vision changes, contact a healthcare professional.

title: Swelling and body changes
content: Mild swelling can occur, especially later in pregnancy. Elevating feet, gentle walking, and hydration may help. If swelling is sudden, painful, or associated with other concerning symptoms, seek medical advice promptly.

title: Nutrition basics in pregnancy
content: Balanced meals support energy and mood. Aim for regular meals with protein, fiber, and fluids. If nausea limits food choices, focus on what you can tolerate and discuss persistent issues with a provider.

title: Hydration and mood
content: Dehydration can worsen fatigue, headaches, and irritability. Keeping a water bottle nearby, adding fruit, or choosing soups and watery foods can help. If you’re vomiting frequently or can’t keep fluids down, reach out to a healthcare professional.

title: Gentle activity for mood support
content: If your provider says activity is safe, gentle movement like walking or prenatal yoga may support mood and sleep. Start small and listen to your body. Stop and seek advice if you feel dizzy, have pain, or experience concerning symptoms.

title: Prenatal yoga and relaxation
content: Prenatal yoga can combine movement, breath, and body awareness—useful for stress and sleep. Choose pregnancy-specific classes and avoid positions that feel uncomfortable. Always follow any guidance from your healthcare provider.

title: Daily routine for steadier mood
content: A simple routine can reduce mental load: regular meals, hydration, brief movement, and a consistent sleep window. Even a 10-minute “anchor habit” (walk, stretch, journal) can create stability on hard days.

title: Relationship stress during pregnancy
content: Pregnancy can change roles, finances, and expectations—stress in relationships is common. Consider using “I” statements, setting small check-in times, and asking for specific support. If you feel unsafe or controlled, reach out to a trusted professional or support service.

title: Work and school stress while pregnant
content: Work and school demands can increase stress and fatigue. If possible, prioritize tasks, take micro-breaks, and communicate accommodations you need (sitting breaks, hydration, flexible timing). If stress feels unmanageable, consider talking with a counselor or provider.

title: Handling worry spirals
content: When worries loop, try a ‘name it’ step: “This is anxiety talking.” Then pick one grounding action—slow breathing, a short walk, or a task that takes 5 minutes. If you need reassurance, write your questions down for your next appointment rather than Googling late at night.

title: Progressive muscle relaxation
content: Progressive muscle relaxation involves tensing and relaxing muscle groups to release tension. Start with your shoulders and hands, then move down the body. Pairing this with slow breathing can help calm your nervous system.

title: Grounding with temperature and touch
content: If you feel overwhelmed, try sensory grounding: hold something cool, wash hands with warm water, or press your feet into the floor while noticing the contact points. Sensory cues can reduce ‘floating’ feelings during anxiety.

title: Connection as a coping strategy
content: Even brief connection can reduce stress. A short voice note, a walk with a friend, or sitting near someone you trust can help your body feel safer. If you don’t feel comfortable reaching out to people you know, peer support groups may help.

title: Perinatal mental health conditions
content: Perinatal mental health conditions include depression and anxiety during pregnancy and after birth. They are common and treatable, and seeking support early often leads to better outcomes. If you notice persistent low mood, panic, or feeling detached, consider speaking with a provider.

title: When to seek professional help
content: Consider professional support if symptoms last more than two weeks, interfere with daily life, or include thoughts of self-harm. Support can include therapy, peer groups, and medical evaluation. You deserve care, not judgment.

title: Common therapy options for anxiety and mood
content: Talk therapies like CBT (cognitive behavioural therapy) can help you notice unhelpful thought patterns and build coping skills. Some people also benefit from interpersonal therapy, especially during major life changes. Ask your provider about options that fit your needs and pregnancy stage.

title: A simple safety plan idea
content: A safety plan can include: warning signs, coping skills that help, people you can contact, and where to get urgent support. Keeping this written down can be useful on bad days. If you’re in immediate danger, seek emergency help.

title: Canada’s 9-8-8 Suicide Crisis Helpline
content: Across Canada, 9-8-8 provides 24/7 crisis support by call or text in English and French. It’s available if you’re thinking about suicide, worried about someone else, or need immediate emotional support.

title: Canadian perinatal mental health resources
content: Canada has specialized perinatal mental health supports, including educational resources and regional programs. If you’re unsure where to start, a family doctor, midwife, or local public health unit can help connect you to services.

title: PSI Canada supports
content: PSI Canada provides education and support for perinatal mental health across Canada, including ways to connect to peer support and resources. It can be a helpful starting point if you feel isolated or unsure what you’re experiencing.

title: Worrying about baby movement
content: Worry about fetal movement is common, especially for first-time parents. If you notice a change that concerns you, it’s reasonable to contact your care provider for guidance rather than sitting alone with worry. Trust your instincts and seek reassurance when needed.

title: Fear of labor and delivery
content: Fear of childbirth is common. Learning what to expect, practicing breathing and coping skills, and discussing a birth plan with your provider can reduce anxiety. Consider asking about pain management options and what support is available during labor.

title: Body image changes in pregnancy
content: Body changes can trigger mixed emotions, especially if you’ve had past struggles with body image. It can help to focus on body function over appearance and to reduce comparisons. If body image distress is intense, talking with a counselor may help.

title: Loneliness and isolation
content: Feeling lonely during pregnancy can happen even if you have people around you. Small steps—joining a peer group, scheduling brief check-ins, or sharing one honest sentence with someone safe—can reduce isolation. You deserve support.

title: Pregnancy after loss or difficult experiences
content: Pregnancy after a loss or difficult prior experience can bring heightened anxiety and grief. It may help to name what you’re carrying, build a support plan for appointments, and seek specialized counseling or peer support. Your feelings are valid.

title: Feeling anxious before an appointment
content: It makes sense to feel nervous before an appointment—waiting and uncertainty can be really stressful. If it helps, try a 60‑second reset: inhale slowly, exhale longer than you inhale, and repeat a few times. You can also write down 1–2 questions you want to ask so you feel more prepared. What part of the appointment is worrying you most right now?
steps: Ask someone you trust to come with you if possible

title: Worry about a glucose test
content: It’s very common to feel anxious before testing—many people worry about ‘what if’ outcomes. A helpful approach is to focus on what you can control: showing up, asking questions, and planning the next step with your care team if needed. Would you like a quick grounding exercise to reduce the worry spiral right now?
steps: List what you can control today
Prepare one question for your provider

title: When everything feels like too much
content: When you’re overwhelmed, your brain is trying to protect you by sounding an alarm—so it can feel hard to think clearly. Let’s shrink the moment: pick one small next step (drink water, sit down, slow breathing, or text someone safe). If you tell me what’s piling up, I can help you choose one manageable step for the next 10 minutes.
steps: Reach out to one supportive person

title: Feeling down during pregnancy
content: I’m sorry you’re feeling this way. Mood changes can happen during pregnancy, and feeling down doesn’t mean you’re failing—it means you need support. If it’s okay, can you share how long you’ve been feeling like this and whether anything specific triggered it? If these feelings are lasting or intense, it can also help to talk with a healthcare professional who supports perinatal mental health.
steps: Do one gentle self‑care action (shower, food, rest)
Share how you’re feeling with someone you trust
Consider speaking to a care provider if it continues

title: If you’re having panic-like symptoms
content: That sounds scary—panic symptoms can feel intense, but they do pass. Try this: breathe in for 3, out for 6, and repeat for a minute. As you breathe out, gently relax your shoulders and jaw. Then look around and name 5 things you can see. Are you safe right now, and are the symptoms easing at all?

title: Racing thoughts at night
content: Racing thoughts can keep your body in ‘alert mode.’ A practical trick is to ‘park’ worries: write them down on paper, then write one tiny action for tomorrow (even ‘ask my doctor’ counts). After that, do something repetitive and calming like slow breathing or a short body scan. What time do you usually notice the worry starting?

title: Stress with a partner or family member
content: Relationship stress can feel heavier during pregnancy because you’re already carrying a lot physically and emotionally. If you want, we can sort this into: what you need, what you can ask for, and what boundary might help. What’s the biggest thing you wish your partner/family understood right now?
steps: Name your need in one sentence
Pick one request you can make today
Plan a calm time to talk

title: Feeling uneasy about body changes
content: Body changes can bring up a mix of emotions—pride, discomfort, worry, or grief. It may help to notice the story your mind is telling (‘I look…’) and replace it with a kinder truth (‘My body is doing something demanding and meaningful.’). What change has been hardest to adjust to?

title: Worry about baby’s movement
content: It’s understandable to feel worried about movement—many parents do. Because your health and your baby’s health are important, it’s best to follow your clinician’s guidance for movement concerns. If you notice a significant change or you’re unsure, consider contacting your care provider for advice. In the meantime, taking a slow breath and writing down what you’ve noticed can help you explain it clearly if you call.
steps: Use slow breathing to reduce panic
Contact a provider if you’re concerned

title: Nausea is wearing you down
content: Nausea can be exhausting—physically and emotionally. Small, frequent snacks, staying hydrated, and gentle rest can help. If nausea is severe or you can’t keep fluids down, it’s important to check in with a healthcare professional. How intense is it today (mild, moderate, or severe)?

title: Pregnancy fatigue support
content: Fatigue in pregnancy is very common, and it can make everything feel harder. If you can, choose one ‘energy-protecting’ action today: a short nap, a slow walk, or asking someone for help with one task. What’s one thing you could take off your plate today?
steps: Ask for help with one chore

title: Headaches and stress
content: Headaches can be linked with stress, sleep, hydration, and many other factors. Try hydration, a calm/dim environment, and gentle neck/shoulder relaxation. If headaches are severe, sudden, or concerning, it’s best to contact a healthcare professional for guidance. Want a 60‑second relaxation routine for your shoulders and jaw?
steps: Contact provider if severe or unusual

title: Back discomfort support
content: Back and hip discomfort can be common as your body changes. Gentle movement, supportive pillows, and light stretching can help. If pain is severe or you’re worried, check in with your provider. Where do you feel it most—lower back, hips, or pelvic area?

title: Heartburn can feel miserable
content: Heartburn can be uncomfortable. Smaller meals, avoiding lying down right after eating, and sleeping slightly elevated can help. If symptoms are frequent or severe, a provider can suggest safe options. What seems to trigger it most—certain foods or late meals?

title: Feeling alone
content: Feeling lonely during pregnancy can be really painful, especially if you feel like others don’t fully get it. You deserve support. If you’re open to it, we can think about one small connection step—message a friend, join a supportive group, or talk with a provider. What kind of support would feel most helpful right now: listening, practical help, or reassurance?
steps: Talk to a provider if feelings persist

title: Irritability and mood swings
content: Irritability can happen when your body is under strain—sleep changes, stress, and hormones can all play a role. A helpful reset is to pause, unclench your jaw, and take three slow exhales before responding. If you want, tell me what’s been setting you off most lately and we can plan one coping step.

title: Worrying about symptoms
content: It’s natural to worry when your body feels different. If you’re concerned about a symptom, it can help to note what you’re feeling (when it started, how intense, what makes it better/worse) and share that with your provider. Emotionally, let’s try to separate ‘possibility’ from ‘probability’—your mind may be jumping to worst‑case scenarios. What symptom is on your mind right now?
steps: Use grounding to reduce ‘what if’ spirals

title: Noticing a positive moment
content: I’m glad you’re feeling this moment of positivity. It can help to ‘bank’ it: write one sentence about what’s going well and what helped create it, so you can come back to it on harder days. What’s been the best part of today so far?
steps: Share the moment with someone you trust

title: Journaling for emotional clarity
content: Writing can help untangle thoughts and reduce mental load. If you want a simple prompt: ‘Right now I feel…, because…, and what I need is…’. Want a guided prompt you can save in your journal?
steps: Use the prompt ‘I feel… because… I need…’
Circle one need you can act on

title: Quick grounding to calm your body
content: Let’s do a quick grounding reset. Look around and name: 5 things you see, 4 things you feel, 3 things you hear, 2 things you smell, and 1 thing you taste. Take one slow breath between each number. When you finish, tell me if your stress feels even 1% lower.
steps: Take one slow breath between steps

title: Work or school stress while pregnant
content: Balancing pregnancy with work or school can be a lot. A helpful approach is to reduce the load where you can: choose the top priority for today and postpone or delegate one smaller thing. If you tell me what’s due, I can help you pick the smallest next step to get momentum.

title: Feeling unheard by healthcare providers
content: Feeling dismissed or not listened to can be really frustrating. You deserve respectful care. It can help to write down your symptoms and questions ahead of time and bring them with you, or ask for clarification in plain language. If you want, tell me what happened and we can draft a short message or question list for your next visit.
steps: Ask for clarification in plain language

title: Fear of becoming a parent
content: A lot of people worry about whether they’ll be a ‘good parent.’ The fact that you care is already a strong sign. Instead of aiming for perfect, aim for ‘good enough’ and supported. What’s one specific fear you have about parenting that we can unpack together?
steps: List 1 support you can lean on

title: Unwanted or intrusive thoughts
content: Unwanted thoughts can be distressing, but having a thought doesn’t mean you want it or that it will happen. A helpful strategy is to label it: ‘This is an intrusive thought,’ then gently bring your attention back to the present (breath, feet on the floor, sounds around you). If these thoughts are frequent or intense, talking with a professional can really help. Would you like a grounding exercise now?

title: Pregnancy after loss or trauma
content: I’m really sorry—pregnancy after loss can bring up a lot of fear and grief. It’s understandable if you’re holding both hope and worry at the same time. Many people find it helpful to have extra support, gentle routines, and someone to talk to. If you want, you can share what feels hardest today and I’ll stay with you through it.
steps: Reach out to a trusted support
